refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires explicit binding to measurable latency acceptance criteria and clarification of what constitutes 'worksite model' (equipment state, environment, events). The refinement anchors the requirement to the p95 metric and customer-defined targets, enabling testable verification. High safety criticality demands that latency bounds protect operator situational awareness and safety decision-making.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor/event generation through worksite model update completion across representative scenarios (equipment movement, obstacle detection, tele-operator command feedback, safety alert propagation). Validate p95 latency against acceptance criteria under nominal and degraded network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency and accuracy to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit equipment state and event data to the worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into the worksite model representation within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to all authorized consumers (operator displays, tele-operator interface, safety services) within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Monitor and report worksite model update latency performance; alert if p95 latency exceeds threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model service with real-time data ingestion pipeline; equipment publishes telemetry at fixed interval; model service processes and broadcasts updates via publish-subscribe.
DP-B: Distributed edge-based model replicas on each vehicle with periodic synchronization to central authority; local updates propagate via gossip protocol.
DP-C: Hybrid architecture: vehicle maintains local model replica for low-latency local queries; central service maintains authoritative model; bidirectional sync with conflict resolution.
DP-D: Event-driven model updates triggered only on state change detection; equipment publishes deltas; model service applies incremental updates and notifies subscribers.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized worksite model service with real-time data ingestion pipeline; equipment publishes telemetry at fixed interval (or on-change with hysteresis); model service processes and broadcasts updates via publish-subscribe to all authorized consumers.

potential_sources: Coupling between telemetry publication frequency and model update latency: higher frequency reduces staleness but increases network load and processing overhead.
Coupling between network link reliability and p95 latency: packet loss or congestion can cause retransmissions and jitter.
Coupling between model service processing capacity and update throughput: insufficient compute can cause queueing delays.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A decouples equipment from model consumers via publish-subscribe, reducing direct dependencies. However, latency is sensitive to publication interval, network conditions, and service processing capacity. These are design parameters that must be tuned during detailed design. The architecture is sound for the functional requirement but requires careful tuning of operational parameters.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network link provides sufficient bandwidth and bounded latency for telemetry publication at required frequency.
Assumption: Worksite model service has sufficient processing capacity to ingest, process, and broadcast updates within latency budget.
Assumption: All consumers can tolerate eventual consistency (brief staleness) rather than strict real-time synchronization.
Risk: If network latency is highly variable or congested, p95 latency may exceed target even with optimized service design.
Risk: If equipment generates telemetry faster than network can carry, buffering and queueing delays will accumulate.
Risk: Single point of failure in centralized model service; no redundancy specified.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) telemetry publication interval or on-change threshold with hysteresis; (2) network link SLA (bandwidth, latency, packet loss); (3) model service processing SLA (ingestion rate, broadcast latency); (4) redundancy and failover strategy for model service. Consider adding a latency monitoring and alerting mechanism (FR-NGHE-007.5) to detect and report degradation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: End-to-end worksite model update pipeline: telemetry ingestion from Vehicle, processing and storage in central model service, and distribution to all consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, operator displays). Includes network transport, model synchronization logic, and latency monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Equipment state (position, orientation, mode) and sensor data from Vehicle; published at fixed interval or on-change; consumed by worksite model service for update processing.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Updated worksite model state distributed to Teleoperation and other consumers for operator display and decision support; includes equipment positions, obstacles, operational status.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical updates (e.g., collision risk, geofence violation) propagated to Safety Services with minimal latency; may trigger automated safety responses.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport for telemetry ingestion and model distribution; latency and reliability of Network Link directly impact p95 update latency.

module_containment_scores.notes: Worksite model update is a core system-level function; NGHE Solution is the natural owner of end-to-end latency performance. All sub-components (telemetry ingestion, model processing, distribution) logically belong within NGHE Solution scope. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link is external dependency.

Vehicle is responsible for telemetry generation and publication, but not for model processing or distribution. Assigning the entire DP to Vehicle would scatter model logic across multiple vehicles, creating high diffusion and inconsistency. Vehicle should own only telemetry publication sub-function (FR-NGHE-007.1).

Teleoperation is a consumer of worksite model updates, not the owner of model maintenance. Assigning model update logic to Teleoperation would couple tele-operator interface to model synchronization, reducing modularity and reusability. Teleoperation should own only model consumption and display (part of FR-NGHE-007.4).

Safety Services consumes safety-critical updates from the worksite model but does not own model maintenance. Assigning model update logic to Safety Services would create tight coupling and duplicate model state. Safety Services should own only safety-specific alert generation and response (part of FR-NGHE-007.5).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of the worksite model update function because: (1) it is the system-level integrator responsible for end-to-end latency performance; (2) it centralizes model state to avoid diffusion and inconsistency; (3) it decouples equipment (Vehicle) from consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services) via publish-subscribe; (4) it enables unified latency monitoring and optimization across all update scenarios.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not explicitly responsible for latency SLA, ownership may be ambiguous and latency degradation may not be detected or addressed.
Risk: If model service is not redundant or resilient, single point of failure could violate latency requirements.
Risk: If Network Link SLA is not contractually bound, network congestion could cause latency to exceed target without clear accountability.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly assign ownership of worksite model update latency SLA to NGHE Solution. Define clear interfaces and SLAs for Telemetry (input from Vehicle), Tasking and SafetyAlert (outputs to consumers), and Network Link (transport). Establish latency monitoring and alerting within NGHE Solution to detect and report degradation. Consider redundancy and failover strategy for model service to ensure resilience.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized model service with publish-subscribe) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for bounded p95 latency. The architecture decouples equipment from consumers, enabling independent optimization of telemetry publication, model processing, and distribution. Latency is a measurable outcome of the DP design and operational parameters (publication interval, network SLA, service capacity). No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If model processing logic is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services instead of centralized in NGHE Solution, latency will be inconsistent and difficult to optimize. Mitigation: assign all model processing to NGHE Solution; Vehicle publishes telemetry only; consumers subscribe to model updates.

Risk: If Network Link SLA is not contractually bound or monitored, network congestion could cause latency to exceed target without visibility. Mitigation: establish explicit Network Link SLA and include latency monitoring in NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for the worksite model update function. It centralizes model state and processing, avoiding diffusion across multiple modules. External dependencies are limited to Telemetry input (from Vehicle), Tasking/SafetyAlert outputs (to consumers), and Network Link transport. These are well-defined interfaces that can be contractually bound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency can be measured end-to-end from telemetry generation to model update completion. Verification requires instrumentation of the telemetry pipeline, model service, and distribution network. Test scenarios should cover nominal conditions, network degradation, and peak load. p95 latency can be computed from latency samples across all scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Ownership of latency SLA is clear: NGHE Solution is responsible for end-to-end latency performance. Sub-functions (telemetry ingestion, model processing, distribution) are allocated to specific sub-modules within NGHE Solution. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and can be contractually bound.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify operational parameters: telemetry publication interval, network link SLA, model service processing SLA, and redundancy strategy.
Establish explicit latency monitoring and alerting mechanism (FR-NGHE-007.5) to detect and report degradation.
Define acceptance criteria for each sub-function (FR-NGHE-007.1 through FR-NGHE-007.5) to enable incremental verification.
Conduct network simulation and load testing to validate that DP-A can meet p95 latency target under all supported scenarios.

decision.justification: The refined FR is decomposable into five atomic sub-functions (FR-NGHE-007.1 through FR-NGHE-007.5) that address distinct aspects of the worksite model update pipeline: telemetry acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution, and monitoring. Each sub-FR requires detailed design and verification. The selected DP (centralized model service with publish-subscribe) is sound at the system level but requires refinement of operational parameters and sub-component design. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will enable detailed specification of telemetry format, network protocol, model processing algorithm, and latency monitoring mechanism.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency and accuracy to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit equipment state and event data to the worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into the worksite model representation within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to all authorized consumers (operator displays, tele-operator interface, safety services) within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Monitor and report worksite model update latency performance; alert if p95 latency exceeds threshold.

assumptions: Network link provides sufficient bandwidth and bounded latency for telemetry publication at required frequency.
Worksite model service has sufficient processing capacity to ingest, process, and broadcast updates within latency budget.
All consumers can tolerate eventual consistency (brief staleness) rather than strict real-time synchronization.
Equipment state changes are infrequent enough that fixed-interval or on-change publication does not overwhelm the network.
Latency is measured end-to-end from telemetry generation to model update completion, not including consumer-side processing.
open_risks: Network latency variability: if network link has high jitter or congestion, p95 latency may exceed target.
Model service scalability: if equipment generates telemetry faster than service can process, queueing delays will accumulate.
Single point of failure: centralized model service has no redundancy; failure would violate latency requirements.
Safety criticality: high safety criticality demands that latency bounds are met reliably; any degradation could impact operator safety.
Customer-defined target: if customer specifies a latency target tighter than 1.0 s, DP-A may require optimization or architectural changes.
evidence_needed: Network link SLA: bandwidth, latency, packet loss, jitter under nominal and degraded conditions.
Model service capacity: ingestion rate, processing latency, broadcast latency under peak load.
Telemetry frequency: equipment state change rate, publication interval, data volume per update.
Consumer latency budget: acceptable staleness for operator display, tele-operator interface, safety services.
Redundancy and failover strategy: how model service failure is detected and mitigated.
Latency measurement methodology: instrumentation points, sampling strategy, p95 computation method.
